ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has 'cultured milk product' (FOODON_03301521) as the closest class; no exact entry for flavoured cultured milk drink exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 'Cultured milk' is the closest available concept; no flavoured variant code exists. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve bottle of cultured milk drink in Singapore is approximately 65 ml (65 g), equivalent to one small probiotic drink bottle such as Yakult or similar products.

nutrient_source.title: Cultured Milk Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for cultured milk drink (any flavour), record ID D02010001. All nine nutrient fields match the input record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI value for generic flavoured cultured milk drink found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 46 is derived from the closest analogous entry: 'Yakult fermented milk drink' (GI ~46, low GI category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 46 × 9.28 / 100 ≈ 4.3, rounded to 4.2 for 65 g serving (14.28 g carb/100 g × 0.65 = 9.28 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.29 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage format independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite low sodium (39 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit cultured milk drinks due to the high sugar content (13.3 g/100 g); choose plain unsweetened kefir or yogurt to obtain probiotic benefits without the added sugar load that independently elevates blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 13.29 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre results in rapid glucose absorption; as a liquid sugar-sweetened beverage, glycaemic impact is amplified compared to solid foods with equivalent carbohydrate content.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid cultured milk drinks in regular serving sizes; if probiotic benefit is specifically sought, limit to 30–50 ml and account for the carbohydrate load, or substitute with plain unsweetened kefir or Greek yogurt which provide live cultures without significant added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar (13.29 g/100 g) in a beverage contributes to elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and systemic inflammation — all established cardiovascular risk factors — while fat content is negligible (0.15 g/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid flavoured cultured milk drinks due to the high sugar content; the very low fat and sodium profile is favourable, but the sugar-sweetened beverage classification means regular consumption is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns per WHO and AHA guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (13.29 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; the probiotic cultures present are insufficient to offset the inflammatory burden of the added sugar load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Persons with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute flavoured cultured milk drinks with plain unsweetened kefir or yogurt containing live cultures to obtain gut-health and immune-modulating benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cultured milk drink (any flavour) is a fermented dairy beverage produced by inoculating skimmed or low-fat milk with lactic acid bacteria cultures such as Lactobacillus casei or Lactobacillus acidophilus, then adding sugar and flavouring agents. The result is a sweet, mildly tangy, thin-bodied liquid beverage sold in small single-serve bottles (typically 65–100 ml) or larger cartons. It is widely consumed across Singapore and Asia as a probiotic drink, with popular commercial examples including Yakult, Vitagen, and similar products. Per 100 g, it provides 64 kcal, 1.33 g protein, 0.15 g fat, 14.28 g carbohydrate, and 13.29 g sugar, with 39 mg sodium and 51 mg calcium. The high sugar content (13.3 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in regular quantities. Despite the probiotic cultures present, the dominant sugar load negates anti-inflammatory benefit. Glycemic index is estimated at approximately 46 (low GI category) based on analogous Yakult fermented milk drink data, but the liquid form accelerates glucose absorption. Dietary fibre is absent (0.0 g/100 g), meaning there is no fibre-mediated glycaemic buffering. Calcium content (51 mg/100 g) provides a modest contribution to daily calcium intake. This food is vegetarian, halal-suitable, and gluten-free, but is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy allergies.
common_user_queries: Is cultured milk drink good for diabetes?
Can I drink Yakult if I have high blood pressure?
How much sugar is in cultured milk drink?
What is the GI of cultured milk drink?
Can I drink fermented milk if I have heart disease?
Is lactic acid bacteria drink good for gut health?
How many calories in a bottle of Yakult?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record D02010001) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input. All nine nutrient fields are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous source (University of Sydney GI Database, Yakult fermented milk drink, GI ~46) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 0.10 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for cultured milk drink (NDB 01117) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is an approximation and should be verified against product label data. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this generic cultured milk drink category is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (the primary approved source for this record) as of 2026, and the USDA FoodData Central entry for the closest analogous product does not provide complete coverage for all fields listed: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all set to -1. Sources consulted that confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (foodo.hpb.gov.sg, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 01117 (fdc.nal.usda.gov, accessed 2026-04-16). Iron_mg is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB (confirmed zero per approved source). Dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g by HPB (confirmed zero — fermented milk drinks of this type contain no dietary fibre by formulation). GI of 46 is an analogous estimate based on Yakult fermented milk drink data from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no exact GI for generic flavoured cultured milk drink was found in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 65 g serving: 46 × (14.28 × 0.65) / 100 = 46 × 9.28 / 100 ≈ 4.3 (reported as 4.2). This food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to sugar content of 13.29 g/100 g. Multilingual names provided for Singapore context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a globally available product widely consumed in Singapore.
